tagged_story_for_tts:
Energy in motion.<[silence:50ms]> The unseen architect of change.<[silence:50ms]>

The word heat traces back to Old English hǣtu, meaning "warmth"—but it’s not just warmth.<[silence:50ms]> Heat is energy racing between atoms, like a crowd jostling in a stadium.<[silence:50ms]> When you rub your hands together, friction forces their atoms to vibrate faster;<[silence:40ms]> that agitation is heat.<[silence:50ms]> A stove burner doesn’t "contain" heat—it pumps energy into your pot’s molecules, making them bounce wildly until your water boils at exactly 212 Fahrenheit (or 100 Celsius at sea level).<[silence:50ms]> No mystery:<[silence:40ms]> faster atoms = hotter matter.<[silence:50ms]>

Heat always flows one way—hot to cold—like a river seeking the lowest ground.<[silence:50ms]> Drop an ice cube in lemonade, and the drink’s energy rushes into the ice, melting it while the lemonade cools.<[silence:50ms]> This rule powers everything:<[silence:40ms]> engines turn heat into motion, fridges steal heat from food, and your body burns calories to stay at 98.6 Fahrenheit, sweating to dump excess.<[silence:50ms]>

Three related subjects are thermodynamics laws, how fire bends metal, and why deserts freeze at night.<[silence:50ms]>
clean_story:
Energy in motion. The unseen architect of change.

The word heat traces back to Old English hǣtu, meaning "warmth"—but it’s not just warmth. Heat is energy racing between atoms, like a crowd jostling in a stadium. When you rub your hands together, friction forces their atoms to vibrate faster; that agitation is heat. A stove burner doesn’t "contain" heat—it pumps energy into your pot’s molecules, making them bounce wildly until your water boils at exactly 212 Fahrenheit (or 100 Celsius at sea level). No mystery: faster atoms = hotter matter.

Heat always flows one way—hot to cold—like a river seeking the lowest ground. Drop an ice cube in lemonade, and the drink’s energy rushes into the ice, melting it while the lemonade cools. This rule powers everything: engines turn heat into motion, fridges steal heat from food, and your body burns calories to stay at 98.6 Fahrenheit, sweating to dump excess.

Three related subjects are thermodynamics laws, how fire bends metal, and why deserts freeze at night.
